questions.question: A Denial of Service (DoS) attack targets which property of the CIA triad?
options: Confidentiality — it prevents unauthorized disclosure of data
Integrity — it ensures data has not been tampered with
Availability — it ensures authorized users can access resources when needed
Non-repudiation — it prevents parties from denying their actions
questions.explanation: The CIA triad consists of Confidentiality (keeping data private), Integrity (keeping data unaltered), and Availability (keeping services accessible). DoS attacks flood or crash a target so that legitimate users cannot reach it — a direct attack on Availability. Non-repudiation is not part of the CIA triad.

questions.question: The primary goal of a DoS attack is to steal or exfiltrate sensitive data from the target.
questions.explanation: DoS attacks are designed to disrupt or deny access to a service, not to steal data. An attacker launches a DoS to make a resource unavailable — for example, crashing a web server so no one can use it. Data theft is associated with different attack types such as SQL injection or phishing.

questions.question: What distinguishes a volumetric DoS attack from an application-level DoS attack?
options: Volumetric attacks exploit software bugs; application-level attacks flood bandwidth
Volumetric attacks saturate network bandwidth with traffic; application-level attacks exploit weaknesses in how an application processes requests
Volumetric attacks only target HTTPS services; application-level attacks only target HTTP
Volumetric attacks always use botnets; application-level attacks always use a single machine
questions.explanation: Volumetric attacks overwhelm the network link itself by sending enormous amounts of traffic (e.g., UDP floods, ICMP floods), consuming all available bandwidth. Application-level attacks instead exploit the logic of an application — for example, sending carefully crafted requests that are cheap to send but expensive for the server to process, crashing or hanging it without needing to saturate the link.

questions.question: In the context of DoS, what is a 'booter' service?
options: A legitimate tool used by network administrators to reboot remote servers
A paid DoS-for-hire service that lets anyone launch an attack against a target
A type of intrusion detection system that reboots itself after an attack
A hardware device that boots compromised machines into a clean OS
questions.explanation: Booters (also called stressers) are underground commercial services that sell DoS attack capability. A customer pays a fee and provides a target IP address; the service then floods that target. They are illegal to use against systems you do not own, and are part of the cybercrime-as-a-service ecosystem.

questions.question: During a SYN flood attack, why does the server run out of resources?
options: The server must decrypt each SYN packet, which is computationally expensive
The server allocates a half-open connection entry for every SYN it receives, and the attacker never completes the handshake, filling up the connection table
Each SYN packet permanently opens a port, and the operating system only supports 65,535 ports
The server must send a SYN-ACK and immediately close the connection, doubling its network load
questions.explanation: TCP requires a 3-way handshake: SYN → SYN-ACK → ACK. When a server receives a SYN, it reserves memory for a 'half-open' connection and sends a SYN-ACK, waiting for the client's ACK. In a SYN flood, the attacker sends thousands of SYNs but never replies with the ACK. The server's connection table fills with these half-open entries waiting to time out, preventing it from accepting legitimate connections.

questions.question: An ICMP-based DoS attack works by:
options: Sending malformed SQL queries via ICMP tunneling to corrupt a database
Flooding a target with ICMP echo request packets faster than it can respond, consuming CPU and bandwidth
Using ICMP redirect messages to reroute all traffic through the attacker's machine
Exploiting the ICMP protocol to decrypt TLS sessions
questions.explanation: ICMP (Internet Control Message Protocol) is used for diagnostics like ping. An ICMP flood sends so many echo request packets that the target is overwhelmed processing them and sending replies, consuming CPU cycles and saturating network bandwidth. This is a volumetric attack that doesn't require exploiting any application vulnerability.

questions.question: What makes a 'permanent DoS' (phlashing) more damaging than a typical DoS attack?
options: It simultaneously attacks all users' browser sessions rather than just the server
It causes irreversible hardware damage — for example, by flashing corrupt firmware — that requires physical replacement or repair
It permanently blocks all IP addresses from reaching the target forever
It encrypts all the target's data with a key only the attacker holds
questions.explanation: Phlashing is a permanent DoS (PDoS) attack that destroys firmware or hardware configuration — for instance, flashing a router or network card with corrupt firmware. Unlike a regular DoS that stops when the attack stops, phlashing can render a device permanently unusable, requiring hardware replacement. The term combines 'phreaking' and 'flashing.'

questions.question: Which of the following best explains why DDoS attacks are harder to defend against than single-source DoS attacks?
options: DDoS attacks use more sophisticated encryption to hide their traffic
DDoS traffic comes from thousands of different IP addresses simultaneously, making IP-based blocking ineffective and the total traffic volume enormous
DDoS attacks always target application-layer vulnerabilities, while DoS only targets the network layer
DDoS attacks are legally permitted in some jurisdictions, limiting the defender's options
questions.explanation: In a single-source DoS, you can block one IP address. In a DDoS, the attack originates from a botnet of thousands or millions of compromised machines (zombies) spread across the globe. You cannot block them all by IP without also blocking legitimate users, and the combined traffic volume can overwhelm even large networks. This distributed nature is exactly what makes DDoS mitigation require specialized services like scrubbing centers.

questions.question: An application-level DoS attack differs from a network-level flood because it:
options: Requires far more bandwidth to execute
Can exhaust server resources by sending a small number of expensive, well-crafted requests rather than raw traffic volume
Only works against servers running on Linux
Always results in permanent data loss on the target server
questions.explanation: Application-level DoS attacks are 'low and slow' — an attacker can send a tiny number of requests that trigger extremely expensive operations on the server (such as complex database queries or file parsing). Because the traffic looks like legitimate application use, it can bypass network-level defenses that look for traffic volume. This makes them harder to detect and requires application-aware defenses.

questions.question: In a DDoS attack, the compromised machines in the botnet (called zombies) are typically aware they are participating in an attack.
questions.explanation: Zombie machines are devices that have been silently infected with malware by a botnet operator. The owners of those machines typically have no idea their devices are being used to flood a target. This is why DDoS attacks are so dangerous — they weaponize unwitting victims' computers as part of the infrastructure.

questions.question: What is the key role of an HTTP proxy server?
options: It terminates TLS connections and re-encrypts them using the server's private key
It sits between clients and web servers, forwarding requests on the client's behalf — enabling features like caching, filtering, and anonymity
It replaces DNS by mapping hostnames to IP addresses within the browser
It prevents XSS by stripping script tags from all responses
questions.explanation: An HTTP proxy is an intermediary: the client sends its request to the proxy, which forwards it to the actual web server. The server's response returns through the proxy to the client. This architecture enables caching (serving stored responses to avoid re-fetching), content filtering, access control, and anonymity (since the server only sees the proxy's IP). Proxies operate at the application layer.

questions.question: An HTTP proxy can cache responses so that repeated requests for the same resource do not need to reach the origin server.
questions.explanation: Caching is one of the primary functions of forward proxies. When a resource is fetched, the proxy stores a copy. Subsequent requests for the same resource can be served from the cache, reducing latency and load on the origin server. This is also why cache poisoning attacks are dangerous — if an attacker can store malicious content in a proxy cache, it gets served to all users.

questions.question: During web server hacking reconnaissance, what is the attacker's primary objective?
options: To immediately exploit a remote code execution vulnerability
To passively gather information about what is exposed and potentially vulnerable before attempting any exploitation
To modify the server's DNS records to redirect users
To install a backdoor before any scanning occurs
questions.explanation: Reconnaissance is the first phase of an attack. The attacker's goal is to understand the target's attack surface — open ports, running services, software versions, misconfigurations — without triggering alarms. This passive (or low-noise active) information gathering informs what exploits to attempt next. Jumping straight to exploitation without recon wastes effort and increases detection risk.

questions.question: What type of information does banner grabbing reveal, and why is it useful to an attacker?
options: It reveals the usernames of logged-in users, letting the attacker target specific accounts
It reveals the server software name and version (e.g., 'Apache 2.4.51'), letting the attacker look up known CVEs for that specific version
It reveals the contents of the server's configuration files by triggering an error
It reveals the private TLS key used by the server
questions.explanation: Banners are messages sent by services when a connection is opened — for example, an HTTP response header might say 'Server: nginx/1.18.0'. Banner grabbing collects these to fingerprint the target. Knowing the exact software version lets the attacker search vulnerability databases (like CVE or Exploit-DB) for known unpatched flaws against that specific version. Tools like Netcat, Telnet, and whatweb automate this.

questions.question: Nmap is used during the reconnaissance phase primarily to:
options: Crack password hashes retrieved from a database dump
Discover which ports are open on a target and which services are running on those ports
Inject malicious payloads into web application forms
Enumerate users within an Active Directory environment
questions.explanation: Nmap (Network Mapper) is a port scanning tool. It sends probes to a range of ports and analyzes responses to determine whether each port is open, closed, or filtered, and often identifies the service and version running. This is foundational reconnaissance — an attacker uses the port map to identify the target's exposed attack surface before deciding which vulnerability to exploit.

questions.question: After fingerprinting a web server and identifying it runs Apache 2.2.14, what would an attacker logically do next?
options: Immediately launch a SYN flood to take it offline
Search vulnerability databases for known, unpatched CVEs affecting Apache 2.2.14 specifically
Install a proxy in front of the server to intercept traffic
Change the server's banner to prevent further fingerprinting
questions.explanation: Once the attacker knows the specific software and version, they search CVE databases or Exploit-DB for vulnerabilities that affect exactly that version. Old software often has publicly documented exploits with available proof-of-concept code. This is why keeping software patched and up-to-date — and suppressing version banners — are both important defensive measures.

questions.question: Fingerprinting a web server requires direct network access to the server — it cannot be done passively from public sources.
questions.explanation: Fingerprinting can be partly passive. Public sources like Shodan index server banners and certificates globally. Archive sites like the Wayback Machine may reveal old software versions. WHOIS and DNS records reveal infrastructure details. Active fingerprinting (direct scanning) is also common, but an attacker can learn a great deal without ever sending a packet directly to the target.

questions.question: HTTP is called a stateless protocol. What does this mean in practice?
options: HTTP connections are always encrypted so no state needs to be stored
Each HTTP request is independent — the server does not inherently remember anything about previous requests from the same client
HTTP only supports GET requests, which carry no state
HTTP servers cannot store session data in any form
questions.explanation: Statelessness means each HTTP request/response cycle is self-contained. The server does not natively remember whether you made a previous request. This is why mechanisms like cookies and session tokens were invented — they artificially add state on top of a stateless protocol. Statelessness simplifies servers (they don't track per-connection history) but requires applications to implement their own session management, which can introduce vulnerabilities.

questions.question: Port 443 is associated with HTTPS. Why does HTTPS use a different port than HTTP (port 80)?
options: HTTPS uses UDP instead of TCP, which requires a different port range
Different port numbers allow firewalls and servers to apply different rules to encrypted vs unencrypted web traffic
Port 443 has a higher bandwidth allocation in networking standards
HTTPS is a newer protocol and all newer protocols must use ports above 400
questions.explanation: Port numbers help network equipment route traffic to the correct service. Running HTTPS on a separate port (443) from HTTP (80) lets firewalls, load balancers, and servers distinguish encrypted from unencrypted web traffic and apply different handling — for example, terminating TLS, enforcing HSTS, or redirecting HTTP to HTTPS. Using separate ports is simply a convention (IANA assignment), not a technical requirement of TCP.

questions.question: OpenVAS is used for which purpose in the context of web server security assessment?
options: Cracking password hashes by running dictionary attacks
Automated vulnerability scanning — probing a target for known security weaknesses and misconfigurations
Capturing and replaying HTTP sessions to test for CSRF vulnerabilities
Generating wordlists for brute-force attacks against login forms
questions.explanation: OpenVAS (Open Vulnerability Assessment System) is an open-source vulnerability scanner. It maintains a database of known vulnerabilities (like Nessus's plugins) and automatically tests a target system for misconfigurations, unpatched software, and known exploitable flaws. It produces a report prioritizing findings by severity. It's used both by defenders (to find and fix weaknesses before attackers do) and by attackers during reconnaissance.

questions.question: Which statement best explains why 'harden before deploying' is a security best practice rather than hardening after deployment?
options: Post-deployment hardening is technically impossible on Linux systems
Systems exposed to the internet while not yet hardened can be compromised before defenses are in place; hardening first closes vulnerabilities before the attack window opens
Hardening after deployment violates most software licensing agreements
Pre-deployment hardening is required by TCP/IP standards
questions.explanation: Every moment a system is exposed with default or insecure settings is an opportunity for attackers. Default configurations often include unnecessary open ports, default credentials, and disabled security features. If you harden before going live, the system is never exposed in a vulnerable state. Post-deployment hardening risks the system being exploited during the window between deployment and hardening completion.

questions.question: CIS Benchmarks provide specific, prescriptive configuration recommendations for hardening operating systems and applications.
questions.explanation: The Center for Internet Security (CIS) publishes benchmarks — detailed documents specifying exact configuration settings for hardening common systems like Linux distributions, Windows, and web servers. For example, a benchmark might specify exactly which services to disable, what permission modes to set on sensitive files, and which sysctl kernel parameters to configure. They are widely used as a baseline for compliance and hardening.

questions.question: Why is disabling unnecessary services an important hardening step, even if those services are not directly exposed to the internet?
options: Unnecessary services slow down system boot times significantly
Every running service is potential attack surface — vulnerabilities in an unused service could still be exploited by an attacker who has already gained some access
Disabled services still consume memory and CPU, degrading performance
Internet standards require that unused ports be closed by firewall rules, not service disabling
questions.explanation: The security principle of 'least functionality' says a system should only run what it needs. Each running service may have vulnerabilities. Even if a service isn't directly reachable from outside, an attacker who gains limited access (e.g., via a web app vulnerability) may pivot and exploit a vulnerable internal service to escalate privileges. Removing unused services eliminates that risk entirely — a vulnerability in something not installed cannot be exploited.

questions.question: When locking down the file system, why is it important to run services as non-root users with minimal permissions?
options: Root users cannot be authenticated by web servers
If the service is compromised, the attacker only gains the limited permissions of that service account rather than full system control
Non-root users have faster file I/O, improving application performance
Linux requires all web server processes to run as non-root for TCP port binding
questions.explanation: The principle of least privilege says processes should have only the permissions they need to function. If a web server process runs as root and an attacker exploits it, they gain root access to the entire system. If it runs as a dedicated low-privilege user (like 'www-data'), an attacker who exploits it is confined to that user's permissions — they cannot read sensitive system files, install software, or modify the OS. The chown command is used to change file and process ownership.

questions.question: What is the purpose of logging in a security context, and why should logs be stored separately from the system they monitor?
options: Logs optimize query performance by caching database responses
Logs create a formal record of access and events used for forensic investigation and detecting attacks; storing them remotely prevents an attacker from deleting evidence after a compromise
Logs are required by HTTP/2 for maintaining stateful connections
Logs replace the need for firewalls by recording blocked traffic
questions.explanation: Logs record what happened on a system — who logged in, what commands were run, what files were accessed, what errors occurred. They are essential for detecting attacks in progress and for forensic investigation after an incident. If logs are stored only on the compromised system, an attacker who gains access can delete them to cover their tracks. Centralizing logs to a separate, write-protected system (like a SIEM) preserves the evidence trail.

questions.question: What is the difference between a security log and a security audit?
options: A log is automated and continuous; an audit is a structured, periodic review validating that security controls meet compliance requirements
A log is performed by humans; an audit is generated automatically by software
Logs apply only to databases; audits apply only to web servers
A log validates policy compliance; an audit records system events
questions.explanation: Logging is the ongoing, automated recording of system events (access attempts, commands, errors). An audit is a structured examination — typically periodic — that reviews logs and system configurations to validate that the system is operating in compliance with policies and regulations. An audit might check whether logging is enabled, whether patches are applied, and whether access controls are correctly set. Logs feed into audits but are not the same thing.

questions.question: Defense-in-depth means relying on multiple layers of security. Why is this better than relying on a single strong control?
options: Multiple security tools automatically communicate with each other to stop attacks faster
If one control fails or is bypassed, additional layers still protect the system — no single point of failure exists
Defense-in-depth reduces the total cost of security because each layer is cheaper than one comprehensive control
It is required by international law for systems that process financial data
questions.explanation: No single security control is perfect. Firewalls can be misconfigured, software has vulnerabilities, and users can be phished. Defense-in-depth (also called layered security) assumes that any individual control may fail, so multiple overlapping controls are deployed: network firewall + host-based firewall + IDS + application input validation + WAF + logging. An attacker must defeat every layer, which greatly increases difficulty and detection likelihood.

questions.question: Why are cookies a security risk if they contain sensitive data that is not encrypted or validated server-side?
options: Cookies are always sent over HTTP even on HTTPS sites
Cookies are stored on the client and can be read, modified, or fabricated by the user or an attacker — so any logic the server executes based on cookie values is untrusted
Cookies are visible to all other websites the user visits
Cookies are automatically deleted after each request, making sessions unreliable
questions.explanation: Cookies are stored in the user's browser, not on the server. A malicious user can use browser developer tools to read or edit cookie values. If an application stores a value like 'role=user' in a cookie and uses it to make authorization decisions without server-side validation, an attacker can simply change it to 'role=admin'. This is cookie manipulation. Mitigations: sign cookies cryptographically (HMAC), encrypt sensitive values, and always re-validate important state server-side.

questions.question: Setting the HttpOnly flag on a cookie prevents the cookie from being accessed by JavaScript, which helps protect against XSS-based cookie theft.
questions.explanation: The HttpOnly cookie attribute instructs the browser not to expose that cookie to JavaScript (document.cookie). Without this flag, an XSS payload can steal session cookies by reading document.cookie and sending them to the attacker. HttpOnly doesn't prevent XSS itself, but it limits what XSS can do — stealing the session cookie to hijack the session is one of the most damaging things an XSS attack can do, and HttpOnly blocks that specific vector.

questions.question: Web server password cracking is defined as checking a password against a commonly used password database. How does this differ from brute force?
options: They are identical — both try every possible password
Dictionary/credential attacks test passwords from a pre-built list of likely passwords; brute force systematically tries every possible combination of characters
Dictionary attacks only work on Windows systems; brute force only works on Linux
Brute force uses stolen databases; dictionary attacks generate passwords mathematically
questions.explanation: A brute-force attack tries every possible combination (aaa, aab, aac … zzz) — exhaustive but slow. A dictionary attack uses a wordlist of real, common passwords ('password', '123456', 'letmein') or previously leaked credentials. Dictionary attacks are far faster because they try only likely passwords first. WPScan uses this approach for WordPress sites — trying common WordPress admin passwords against the login form.

questions.question: Why does using an uncommon username (rather than 'admin') help defend against password cracking attacks?
options: Uncommon usernames require stronger encryption algorithms
Password cracking tools typically assume default usernames like 'admin' or 'root' — an unusual username means the attacker must guess both the username and password, multiplying the search space
Web servers do not process login requests for usernames shorter than 8 characters
Uncommon usernames trigger automatic account lockouts after the first failed attempt
questions.explanation: Credential attacks require knowing both the username and the password. Tools like WPScan default to trying obvious usernames ('admin', 'administrator', 'root'). If the admin account uses the username 'x7j2qm', the attacker cannot simply target 'admin'. The attacker must now also guess or enumerate the username, which significantly expands the attack difficulty. This is paired with strong passwords and account lockout for a layered defense.

questions.question: Providing ongoing vulnerability scanning means:
options: Running a scan once during initial deployment and never repeating it
Scheduling periodic automated scans to detect newly discovered vulnerabilities, misconfigurations, and patch gaps over time
Only scanning after a known breach has occurred
Manually reviewing source code once per year for security issues
questions.explanation: New vulnerabilities (CVEs) are disclosed constantly. A system that was fully hardened and scanned clean last month may be vulnerable today if new flaws are discovered in its software. Ongoing vulnerability scanning — using tools like Nessus, OpenVAS, or Qualys on a scheduled basis — ensures the security posture is continuously validated, not just at a single point in time. It is a core practice of a mature vulnerability management program.

questions.question: Nessus is categorized as which type of security tool?
options: A network packet sniffer that captures and decodes traffic
A vulnerability scanner that probes systems for known weaknesses and generates prioritized reports
A password cracker that runs dictionary attacks against login forms
A web application firewall (WAF) that blocks malicious HTTP requests
questions.explanation: Nessus is one of the most widely used vulnerability scanners. It has a large plugin database of known vulnerability checks — it connects to a target, probes its services, and checks for unpatched software, misconfigured settings, and default credentials. It produces reports sorted by severity (Critical, High, Medium, Low). It is used by both defenders for security assessments and attackers during reconnaissance.

questions.question: Defense-in-depth assumes that if the perimeter firewall is strong enough, no other security controls are necessary.
questions.explanation: Defense-in-depth explicitly rejects the idea that any single control is sufficient. A perimeter firewall is valuable but can be bypassed — for example, via a phishing email that establishes an outbound connection, or via a vulnerability in an internet-facing web application the firewall must allow through. Defense-in-depth adds host firewalls, network segmentation, IDS/IPS, application security controls, and monitoring so each layer compensates for the others' weaknesses.

questions.question: An attacker modifies the value of a cookie named 'discount=0' to 'discount=100'. What class of vulnerability does this exploit?
options: SQL injection — the attacker is modifying a database query via the cookie
Cookie manipulation — the application trusts client-supplied cookie values without server-side validation
XSS — the attacker is injecting a script via the cookie
CSRF — the attacker is forging a cross-site request using the modified cookie
questions.explanation: This is a cookie manipulation attack. Cookies are stored on the client and can be freely edited. If the server reads 'discount=100' from the cookie and applies a 100% discount without verifying that the user is entitled to it server-side, the attacker gets an unauthorized discount. The mitigation is never to trust client-supplied values for security-sensitive decisions — always validate or recalculate on the server.

questions.question: Storing a user's role (e.g., 'admin' or 'user') in an unencrypted, unsigned cookie is a safe practice because the browser prevents users from modifying cookies.
questions.explanation: Browsers do not prevent users from editing their own cookies. Browser developer tools make it trivial to view and modify cookies. Storing 'role=user' unencrypted and unsigned means any user can change it to 'role=admin' and potentially gain administrative access. Roles and permissions should be stored server-side (in a session store keyed by a signed session token), not in an editable client-side cookie.

questions.question: Which mitigation most effectively prevents cookie manipulation attacks?
options: Increase the cookie expiry time so the session lasts longer
Store security-sensitive data server-side in a session store referenced by a cryptographically signed session ID, rather than in client-readable cookie values
Use the SameSite cookie attribute set to 'None' to allow cross-site sharing
Hash the cookie name so attackers cannot find it in developer tools
questions.explanation: The fundamental fix for cookie manipulation is to not put sensitive data in client-editable cookies. Instead, issue the client a session ID (a long random string stored in a signed cookie). All actual session data (user ID, role, cart contents) is stored in a server-side session store indexed by that ID. The client never sees or can modify the actual data. Even if they forge a session ID, it won't correspond to a valid server-side session.

questions.question: Why does WPScan specifically target WordPress installations?
options: WordPress encrypts passwords differently from other platforms
WordPress's known user enumeration weaknesses and large plugin ecosystem mean there are many WordPress-specific attack vectors and known vulnerabilities that a specialized scanner can target
WPScan only works on port 443
WordPress sites always use the same default password
questions.explanation: WPScan is a WordPress-specific security scanner. WordPress has a large market share and a vast ecosystem of third-party plugins, many of which have known CVEs. WordPress also has default behaviors — like exposing usernames via the author archive — that attackers exploit. WPScan enumerates WordPress-specific elements: usernames (via /?author=1), plugins, themes, and known vulnerabilities for each, making it a very targeted recon and assessment tool.

questions.question: Which of the following best describes what the 'chown' command does in the context of web server hardening?
options: It changes the permissions of a file (read/write/execute bits)
It changes the owner and/or group of a file or directory, typically to reassign it from root to a less-privileged service account
It changes the hostname of the server
It encrypts a file using the owner's public key
questions.explanation: chown (change owner) reassigns file or directory ownership. For example, 'chown www-data:www-data /var/www/html' makes the web server's files owned by the 'www-data' service account rather than root. This matters because a process can only modify files it owns (or has permission to modify). Running a web server as a non-root user and ensuring it only owns what it needs follows least privilege — a compromised service cannot modify system files it doesn't own.

questions.question: Comments left in publicly accessible HTML or JavaScript source code can represent a security vulnerability because:
options: Comments increase page load time and are blocked by web application firewalls
Comments may contain developer notes with sensitive details like internal paths, API keys, credentials, or logic descriptions that attackers can read by viewing page source
HTML comments are executed by some browsers as JavaScript
Comments in JavaScript prevent minification, exposing the raw code to the public
questions.explanation: HTML comments (<!-- -->) and JavaScript comments (// or /* */) are visible to anyone who views page source. Developers sometimes leave notes like '<!-- TODO: remove hardcoded API key: sk-live-xxx -->' or '// admin panel is at /secret-admin'. An attacker doing reconnaissance will always view page source looking for such leaks. The mitigation is simple: never put sensitive information in comments, and strip comments from production code using minifiers.
